data.title: Topic 1.1.1: Four Chambers of the Heart
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Identify the four chambers of the heart. 
●	Explain the function of each chamber. 
●	Differentiate right-sided and left-sided heart circulation. 
●	Understand how chamber anatomy supports normal blood flow. 
●	Build foundational knowledge for future pacing and electrophysiology topics.

data.title: Topic 1.1.2: Blood Flow Pathways
data.description:
The cardiovascular system functions through a continuous, organized movement of blood known as blood circulation. The heart acts as a dual pump that keeps blood moving in one direction through the lungs and the rest of the body. This movement is called the blood flow pathway.
Blood carries oxygen, nutrients, hormones, immune cells, and heat to body tissues. It also removes carbon dioxide and metabolic waste products. For the body to survive, blood must circulate continuously and efficiently.
The pathway of blood flow is divided into two major circuits:

data.title: Topic 1.1.3: Heart Wall Layers
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Identify the major layers of the heart wall. 
●	Explain the structure and function of each layer. 
●	Differentiate endocardium, myocardium, epicardium, and pericardium. 
●	Understand how heart wall anatomy supports contraction, protection, and circulation. 
●	Recognize clinical conditions affecting each layer.

data.title: Topic 1.1.4: Great Vessels of the Heart
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Identify the major great vessels connected to the heart. 
●	Explain the direction of blood flow through each vessel. 
●	Differentiate arteries from veins in cardiac circulation. 
●	Describe the relationship between chambers and great vessels. 
●	Understand the clinical importance of great vessels in cardiovascular disease and procedures.

data.title: Topic 1.2.1: Overview of Cardiac Valves
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Identify the four valves of the heart. 
●	Explain how valves maintain one-way blood flow. 
●	Differentiate atrioventricular valves from semilunar valves. 
●	Describe valve opening/closing during the cardiac cycle. 
●	Recognize common consequences of valve dysfunction.

data.title: Topic 1.2.2: Tricuspid Valve
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Identify the anatomical location of the tricuspid valve. 
●	Explain its structure and normal function. 
●	Describe how it regulates right-sided blood flow. 
●	Recognize common tricuspid valve disorders. 
●	Understand clinical relevance of tricuspid pathology.

data.title: Topic 1.3.1 - Diastole
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define heart mechanical function in simple and clinical terms. 
●	Explain how the heart works as a pump. 
●	Differentiate electrical activity from mechanical activity. 
●	Describe the major components of cardiac pumping performance. 
●	Understand why mechanical function is essential before learning systole and diastole.

data.title: Topic 1.3.2: Systole
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define systole in simple and clinical terms. 
●	Describe the sequence of events during ventricular contraction. 
●	Explain valve movements during systole. 
●	Understand pressure generation and blood ejection. 
●	Recognize common causes of impaired systolic function.

data.title: Topic 1.3.3: Diastole II
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define diastole in simple and clinical terms. 
●	Describe the phases of ventricular relaxation and filling. 
●	Explain valve movement during diastole. 
●	Understand the importance of ventricular compliance and filling pressures. 
●	Recognize common causes of diastolic dysfunction.

data.title: Topic 1.3.4: Cardiac Cycle
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define the cardiac cycle. 
●	Describe the complete sequence of one heartbeat. 
●	Integrate electrical events, valve motion, pressure changes, and blood flow. 
●	Relate heart sounds to cardiac cycle phases. 
●	Understand why cycle timing is clinically important.

data.title: Topic 1.3.5: Stroke Volume / Cardiac Output / Ejection Fraction
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define stroke volume, cardiac output, and ejection fraction. 
●	Explain how these measurements describe pump performance. 
●	Understand the relationship between heart rate and output. 
●	Recognize normal versus reduced values conceptually. 
●	Apply these concepts to common clinical scenarios.

data.title: Topic 2.1.1: Overview of Cardiac Conduction System
data.description:
Cardiac electrophysiology is the study of the electrical processes that generate and regulate the heartbeat. Every heartbeat begins with an electrical impulse that travels through a specialized conduction system, stimulating the myocardium to contract in a synchronized manner. Proper electrical conduction is essential for maintaining effective cardiac output, rhythm stability, and coordinated chamber contraction.
Understanding electrophysiology is fundamental in cardiac rhythm management because pacemakers, ICDs, CRT devices, and electrophysiology procedures all interact directly with the heart’s electrical system.

data.title: Topic 2.1.2: Sinoatrial (SA) Node
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define the Sinoatrial (SA) Node and explain its role as the natural pacemaker of the heart. 
●	Describe the anatomical location and structure of the SA node. 
●	Explain how the SA node generates electrical impulses through automaticity. 
●	Understand the intrinsic firing rate of the SA node and its dominance over other pacemaker tissues. 
●	Relate SA node dysfunction to clinical conditions and pacemaker indications.

data.title: Topic 2.1.3: Atrioventricular (AV) Node
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define the Atrioventricular (AV) Node and explain its role in the cardiac conduction system. 
●	Describe the anatomical location and structure of the AV node. 
●	Explain the physiological purpose of AV nodal delay and why it is critical for cardiac efficiency. 
●	Understand the AV node’s backup pacemaker function and intrinsic firing rate. 
●	Relate AV nodal dysfunction to clinical arrhythmias and pacemaker indications.

data.title: Topic 2.1.4: Bundle of His
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define the Bundle of His and explain its function within the cardiac conduction system. 
●	Describe the anatomical location and structural pathway of the Bundle of His. 
●	Understand how the Bundle of His conducts impulses from the AV node to the ventricles. 
●	Explain the relationship between the Bundle of His and bundle branches. 
●	Recognize the clinical significance of His bundle conduction abnormalities and their role in device therapy. 
●	Understand the relevance of His-bundle pacing in modern pacing therapy.

data.title: Topic 2.1.5: Bundle Branches and Purkinje Fibers
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define the bundle branches and Purkinje fibers and explain their role in ventricular conduction. 
●	Describe the anatomical pathway of the right and left bundle branches. 
●	Understand how the Purkinje network distributes impulses through the ventricles.

data.title: Topic 2.2.1: Depolarization vs Repolarization
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define depolarization and repolarization in cardiac electrophysiology. 
●	Understand the relationship between electrical activity and myocardial contraction/relaxation. 
●	Correlate depolarization and repolarization with ECG waveforms. 
●	Relate these concepts to pacemaker sensing, pacing capture, and arrhythmia recognition.

data.title: Topic 2.2.2: Impulse Propagation
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define impulse propagation and explain its role in cardiac electrophysiology. 
●	Describe how electrical impulses travel from one cardiac cell to another. 
●	Understand the mechanisms of cell-to-cell conduction and gap junction function. 
●	Explain the concept of conduction velocity and why it varies in different cardiac tissues.

data.title: Topic 2.2.3: Action Potential and Cardiac Cellular Electrophysiology
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define cardiac action potential and explain its importance in heart rhythm generation. 
●	Describe the phases of the cardiac action potential in myocardial cells. 
●	Understand the role of sodium, potassium, and calcium ions in cardiac electrophysiology. 
●	Differentiate between fast-response and slow-response action potentials. 
●	Explain how action potentials relate to depolarization, repolarization, and refractory periods. 
●	Recognize the clinical and device relevance of action potential abnormalities in arrhythmias and pacing therapy.

data.title: Topic 2.2.4: Refractory Periods and Excitability
data.description:
Learning Objective
By the end of this topic, learners will be able to:
●	Define refractory period and explain its role in cardiac electrophysiology. 
●	Differentiate between absolute refractory period, relative refractory period, and effective refractory period. 
●	Explain the concept of cardiac excitability and myocardial responsiveness to electrical stimulation. 
●	Understand how refractory periods prevent abnormal repetitive contraction and arrhythmias. 
●	Relate refractory period concepts to pacing, sensing, ICD therapy, and arrhythmia mechanisms. 
●	Apply refractory period knowledge in device programming and troubleshooting.
